steps.title: Haematology and oncology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Secondary Care

steps.title: Non-Trauma Bleeding
steps.text: A 4-year-old child presents with spontaneous bruising and mucosal bleeding. There is no history of trauma. What is the most appropriate first-line investigation at secondary level to assess the cause?
steps.rationale: At the secondary level, investigation of non-trauma bleeding includes blood work such as FBC, coagulation tests, and factor assays to determine possible bleeding disorders.

textChoices.text: Full blood count and coagulation profile

steps.title: Non-Trauma Bleeding
steps.text: A child with suspected bleeding disorder presents with pallor and tachycardia. You initiate IV access and start investigations. What is the correct next step in management?
steps.rationale: Children with active or significant bleeding may require urgent volume and blood replacement before referral. Whole blood transfusion at 20 mL/kg is recommended at secondary level.

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: Which of the following patient groups is at highest risk of developing Tumour Lysis Syndrome (TLS)?
steps.rationale: TLS commonly occurs in patients with rapidly dividing tumours such as acute leukaemia or high-grade lymphomas, especially when tumour burden is high.

textChoices.text: A child with iron deficiency anaemia
A child with high tumour burden from acute leukaemia
A child with mild viral upper respiratory infection
A child post-surgical removal of benign tumour

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: What is the most appropriate initial fluid management for a child at risk of TLS at a secondary level facility?
steps.rationale: Potassium-free fluids like normal saline are used to avoid exacerbating hyperkalaemia, a key concern in TLS.

steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: Which of the following is the most important initial step in the secondary level management of a child with spinal cord compression?
steps.rationale: Stabilization of airway, breathing, and circulation remains the cornerstone of emergency care at all levels before advanced diagnostics or interventions.

steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: In addition to IV fluids and ABC support, what key supportive intervention should be done early at secondary level care in a patient with spinal cord compression?
steps.rationale: Spinal cord compression often leads to bladder dysfunction. Catheterization helps monitor output and prevent bladder overdistension or urinary retention.

steps.title: Superior Vena Cava Compressions Syndrome
steps.text: What is the most appropriate patient position for a child suspected of having Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Compression Syndrome?
steps.rationale: Lying flat can worsen respiratory distress in SVC syndrome. Elevating the upper body improves venous drainage and reduces upper body congestion.

steps.title: Superior Vena Cava Compressions Syndrome
steps.text: A child presents with facial swelling, distended neck veins, and difficulty breathing. What is the most appropriate next step after securing the airway, breathing, and circulation?
steps.rationale: Tertiary care is needed for imaging, diagnosis, and definitive treatment (e.g., chemotherapy, surgery, or radiotherapy). Early referral prevents delays in life-saving interventions.

textChoices.text: Refer urgently to a tertiary centre
Place the child in a supine position

steps.title: Hyperleukocytosis
steps.text: In managing a child with hyperleukocytosis at the secondary level, what is the recommended intravenous fluid management?
steps.rationale: Aggressive hydration is essential to prevent complications like tumor lysis syndrome. The recommended approach is IV normal saline with 5% dextrose at twice the maintenance rate, avoiding potassium-containing fluids.

textChoices.text: Start Ringer’s lactate at maintenance rate
Start IV fluids with potassium chloride
Start normal saline without dextrose at double maintenance rate
Start IV normal saline with 5% dextrose at 2 times the maintenance rate

steps.title: Wilms Tumour / Nephroblastoma
steps.text: What is the most appropriate next step in the management of a child diagnosed with Wilms tumour at the secondary care level?
steps.rationale: At the secondary level, the focus is on stabilizing the patient through nutritional support, offering realistic counselling on the diagnosis and the expected long course of treatment, and ensuring timely referral to a tertiary center for specialized care including surgery and oncology input.

textChoices.text: Schedule a biopsy to confirm histological subtype
Prescribe oral steroids for tumour reduction

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: What is the most appropriate next step at the primary level when a child presents with leukocoria (white pupillary reflex)?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, a child with suspected retinoblastoma (e.g., leukocoria) must be urgently referred after counselling and discussion with an ophthalmologist at a secondary facility.

textChoices.text: Start topical antibiotics and monitor for improvement
Counsel the caregiver, discuss with ophthalmology
Prescribe vitamin A and review in two weeks
Reassure the family and advise regular eye checks

steps.title: Lymphoma
steps.text: In a child with suspected lymphoma presenting at a secondary care facility, which of the following interventions is contraindicated prior to referral and specialist input?
steps.rationale: Invasive procedures in suspected lymphoma cases should be avoided at secondary level due to potential complications such as hemorrhage or tumor dissemination. They should be performed at tertiary level under controlled and specialized settings.

textChoices.text: Initiating full maintenance potassium-free IV fluids
Stabilizing the patient using the ABC approach
Performing invasive diagnostic procedures such as biopsy or lumbar puncture
Consulting with the tertiary care team regarding the patient’s condition

steps.title: Lymphoma
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial fluid management for a child with suspected lymphoma at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: In a child with suspected lymphoma, potassium-free fluids such as normal saline should be started at full maintenance to avoid complications like tumor lysis syndrome. Potassium is avoided due to the risk of hyperkalemia in these patients.

textChoices.text: Normal saline at full maintenance without potassium

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A 15-month-old child with SCD has no prior treatment. What clinical step is essential before initiating hydroxyurea?
steps.rationale: Initiation of hydroxyurea requires basic lab screening to confirm safe bone marrow and liver/kidney function.

textChoices.text: Administer first dose of penicillin V

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child on hydroxyurea for 6 weeks has ANC of 0.8 x 10⁹/L. What is the most appropriate action?
steps.rationale: ANC <1.0 is a red flag for neutropenia. Withhold HU, monitor, and adjust dose after recovery.

textChoices.text: Withhold the drug and monitor counts

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: Which lab value indicates hydroxyurea can be safely continued without dose change?
steps.rationale: These values are within safe ranges. ALT >2x and platelets <80 are thresholds for withholding HU.

textChoices.text: Hb = 7 g/dL, ANC = 1.8 x 10⁹/L, Platelets = 120 x 10⁹/L

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A 3-year-old child with SCD and no vaccine history should receive how many doses of PCV13?
steps.rationale: Children aged 2–5 with no history of PCV13 should receive 2 catch-up doses spaced by 8 weeks.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: Which situation requires urgent blood transfusion in a child with SCD?
steps.rationale: ACS with hypoxia is an emergency. Blood transfusion helps oxygen delivery and reverses sickling.

textChoices.text: Acute chest syndrome with O₂ saturation < 92%

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A 4-year-old with SCD and sudden abdominal swelling, pallor, and tachycardia is likely experiencing:
steps.rationale: Classic signs of acute splenic sequestration—life-threatening due to hypovolemia and Hb drop.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child with SCD and Hb = 5.2 g/dL has chest pain and new infiltrate on CXR. Best initial management?
steps.rationale: This is acute chest syndrome with anemia. Transfusion improves oxygen-carrying capacity rapidly.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child with SCD has severe bone pain and poor response to ibuprofen and paracetamol. Next step?
steps.rationale: WHO recommends stepwise pain ladder. Severe pain warrants escalation to opioids under supervision.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: What distinguishes a pain crisis from osteomyelitis in a febrile SCD child?
steps.rationale: Osteomyelitis often has localized findings, imaging changes, and poor pain response—unlike typical crises.

textChoices.text: Bone tenderness, positive X-ray, poor response to analgesia

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A 7-year-old with SCD and temperature of 40°C, pallor, and poor perfusion should:
steps.rationale: Red flags include high fever, pallor, and perfusion issues—signs of sepsis, needing urgent admission.

textChoices.text: Receive IV fluids and be admitted immediately

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child with Hb 6.2 g/dL and stroke symptoms requires:
steps.rationale: Acute stroke needs urgent blood transfusion to reduce HbS% and restore oxygenation—life-saving.

textChoices.text: Oral penicillin and review in 1 day
Manual exchange transfusion or simple transfusion

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: In acute chest syndrome with O₂ sats <92% on room air, what is the transfusion goal?
steps.rationale: Transfuse to around 10 g/dL. Higher Hb can increase viscosity and worsen symptoms.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: What is the most reliable early sign of treatment adherence with hydroxyurea?
steps.rationale: HU raises MCV due to its effect on red cell production. A helpful marker of adherence and effect.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child on HU develops toxicity at the current dose. What’s the next step if counts don’t recover in 2 weeks?
steps.rationale: Persistent toxicity after 2 weeks of withholding HU indicates need for dose reduction—not discontinuation.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child with SCD has a seizure and right-sided weakness. What is the immediate first action?
steps.rationale: In suspected stroke, immediate stabilization of airway, breathing, and circulation (ABCCC) is top priority.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A child with Hb of 8.5 g/dL and no signs of severe illness should be treated with:
steps.rationale: For non-severe anaemia, the guideline recommends starting iron and reassessing in 2 weeks.

textChoices.text: Oral iron and follow-up in 14 days

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: In non-severe anaemia, what additional treatment is recommended if the child is ≥1 year and hasn't received it in 6 months?
steps.rationale: Children ≥1 year should receive one dose of mebendazole (500 mg) if not given in the past 6 months.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which child meets the criteria for immediate blood transfusion at secondary level?
steps.rationale: Any child with Hct 13–18% (Hb 4–6 g/dL) plus clinical signs like laboured breathing needs transfusion.

textChoices.text: Hb = 3.8 g/dL, alert and feeding well

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: When using packed red cells for transfusion in a child, the correct volume is:
steps.rationale: Packed cells are preferred and should be given slowly to reduce overload risk.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: What should be monitored every 15 minutes during transfusion?
steps.rationale: Frequent monitoring helps detect signs of heart failure or fluid overload early.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: In children with severe acute malnutrition, how should transfusion be adjusted?
steps.rationale: In severe malnutrition, fluid overload risk is high. Give 10 ml/kg and avoid >20 ml/kg in 4 days.

textChoices.text: Give 10 ml/kg slowly, avoid exceeding 20 ml/kg in 4 days

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A child who doesn't respond to 6 weeks of oral iron therapy should be:
steps.rationale: If there’s no response after 6–8 weeks of iron, referral is required to investigate causes or switch therapy.

textChoices.text: Given more iron for 2 more weeks

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A 7-year-old female with heavy menstrual bleeding and anaemia should be:
steps.rationale: Menorrhagia (heavy periods) should prompt a gynaecology referral to treat the underlying cause.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which of the following is a correct indication to refer a child with anaemia to a tertiary centre?
steps.rationale: Anaemia with additional haematological abnormalities requires specialist evaluation.

textChoices.text: Hb = 8.9 g/dL after 1 week of iron
Anaemia with abnormal WBC and platelets

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A 4-year-old child with Hb = 8.7 g/dL and no severe acute malnutrition is seen at a tertiary centre. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?
steps.rationale: Hb < 9.3 g/dL without complications should be managed with oral iron and a dose of mebendazole if not given in 6 months.
